role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1252 Software Developers 23.9% + 15-1299 Computer Occupations All Other 3.1% + 15-1253 Software QA 2.2% at 4-digit 5415 — for this residual code the mix leans toward deployment/installation and recovery engineers rather than product developers
role_hints.note: Installation scripting, configuration, migration runbooks, DR plan documentation and testing automate heavily; on-site deployment hands, hardware handling and client-specific recovery orchestration judgment persist.

role_hints.oews_basis: 15-1232 Computer User Support Specialists 3.3% at 4-digit; likely higher here given installation/support-service firms, est. ~6-9%
role_hints.note: Install support, troubleshooting and ticket work — directly AI-automatable except the physical/on-site slice.
Assessments, DR/continuity plans and audit documentation automate substantially; client governance persists.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-3021 Computer & Information Systems Managers 8.3% + 13-1082 Project Management Specialists 3.0%
role_hints.note: Rollout coordination and status reporting automate; client escalation persists.
Owner-held client relationships persist; quoting and proposal work automates.

role_hints.oews_basis: remainder beyond the listed top-10 (top-10 sums to ~61% at 4-digit), est. ~28-32%
role_hints.note: Back office automates; part of the tail is field/bench technicians (physical) — allocate between patterns.

source_hints.source: Kaseya Global MSP Benchmark Report / ConnectWise IT Nation research
source_hints.why: Many firms coded 541519 operate as MSP-adjacent IT services shops; MSP-industry surveys are the closest adoption evidence, with the NAICS-frame caveat noted.

source_hints.source: Disaster-recovery-as-a-service (DRaaS) market coverage by Gartner/analyst firms
source_hints.why: The DR sub-segment's pricing shift from consulting engagements to subscription DRaaS is the key capture/deflation fact if DR dominates the firm's mix.

source_hints.source: Solganick / Software Equity Group IT-services M&A reports
source_hints.why: Lower-middle-market IT services deal multiples; firms from this residual code appear in the same deal pools as 541511/541512.

source_hints.source: No dedicated owner-demographic source known for this code
source_hints.why: Residual-code populations are not separately surveyed; fall back on ABS owner-age data or MSP-industry proxies, flagged as such.

capture_questions: Firms in this residual code bill as T&M installation/deployment projects, per-incident or subscription DR/continuity fees, or resale-plus-services bundles — for the dominant sub-segment, when AI cuts delivery hours, does the billing form (hourly project work especially) pass savings straight to clients via competitive quotes, and is subscription DR revenue instead being absorbed by DRaaS platform vendors upstream?

Who owns the client relationship — recurring-contract firms (DR retainers, support agreements) hold institutional relationships with switching costs, while project/installation shops rebid every job; what is the recurring vs. project revenue split in the target tier?

What stops clients from using AI plus vendor-native tooling to self-serve — software installation is increasingly zero-touch by design (SaaS, autodeployment), and cloud platforms bundle DR natively; is the code's addressable base structurally shrinking regardless of firm-level AI adoption?

Where in this heterogeneous code does durable accountability revenue exist (compliance-driven DR attestations, regulated-industry continuity requirements), and how large is it relative to the commodity installation work?
terminal_value_question: This is a residual code whose two visible sub-segments face opposite mechanisms: installation/deployment services are being deleted by zero-touch software distribution (no AI needed — the product is disappearing by design), while disaster-recovery/continuity services migrate from paid human planning to platform-native DRaaS features. The question the run must answer per-sub-segment: is there any surviving paid service here that mature AI strengthens (accountable recovery orchestration for regulated clients) rather than replaces — and is the dominant revenue stream of the code's actual firms on the surviving or the disappearing side?
special_notes: Catch-all/residual code: identify the top-3 revenue sub-segments, score the DOMINANT one, and set heterogeneous: true. Hypothesis (flagged as such): general small IT services shops (MSP-adjacent, installation/support/resale-blended) dominate the firm count, with disaster recovery and specialized computer services as smaller slices — the modal firm here looks like a less-MRR-mature cousin of the 541512 MSP.

Sibling boundaries: 541519 is what remains after 541511 (custom programming), 541512 (systems design/integration — where most self-described MSPs sit) and 541513 (client-facility operations) — NAICS assigns residual and mixed shops here, so the code's population is defined by classification noise; MSP benchmarks and deal data will not distinguish it from 541512, and any firm-level data should be checked for which sibling it really describes.
labor_share 0.4958 signals meaningful pass-through (hardware/software resale) in receipts — normalize revenue mix before margin or multiple work, as with 541512.

n_band 705 (20-99 class: 526 + 100-499: 179) — a real pool, but roll-up buyers (the MSP platforms) screen on MRR quality, and residual-code firms skew project/resale-heavy; expect the acquirable-on-MSP-terms subset to be a fraction of n_band.
The 2022 receipts vintage predates AI service-desk tooling and the DRaaS mainstreaming — treat as a pre-shift baseline.
